title: 10 best herbal teas for phlegm
intro_desc: Herbal teas for phlegm are infusions made from various plants and herbs that help to alleviate symptoms of respiratory ailments, such as congestion, coughing, and excessive mucus production. These teas contain natural anti-inflammatory, expectorant, and soothing properties that work to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, thin out mucus, and soothe irritation. Examples of herbal teas that help with phlegm include thyme, eucalyptus, licorice root, ginger, and slippery elm. By drinking these teas, individuals can experience relief from respiratory discomfort, allowing them to breathe easier and sleep better. For instance, a study published in the Journal of Ethnopharmacology found that thyme tea was effective in reducing the severity and duration of cough and mucus production in patients with acute bronchitis.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marshmallow tea helps with phlegm because it contains mucilage, a type of soluble fiber that becomes slippery when mixed with water. This slippery substance coats the throat and soothes irritation, reducing the urge to cough and clear phlegm. Additionally, marshmallow tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel phlegm. The tea also has expectorant properties, which can help loosen and thin mucus, making it easier to cough up and expel. Overall, marshmallow tea can provide relief for people suffering from respiratory conditions such as bronchitis, coughs, and colds.
remedy_properties: Demulcent: Marshmallow tea is rich in mucilage, which forms a soothing film over the mucous membranes, providing relief from irritation and reducing phlegm production.
Expectorant: The mucilage in marshmallow tea also has expectorant properties, helping to loosen and expel phlegm from the respiratory tract, making it easier to cough up and expel.

Anti-inflammatory: Marshmallow tea contains flavonoids and phenolic acids that exhibit anti-inflammatory effects, reducing inflammation in the respiratory tract and alleviating symptoms associated with phlegm.
Antitussive: The soothing effect of marshmallow tea on the respiratory tract can help suppress coughing, which is often triggered by the presence of phlegm.

Antimicrobial: Marshmallow tea has antimicrobial properties due to the presence of tannins and other compounds, which can help prevent infections that may exacerbate phlegm production and respiratory issues.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 quart of water, 2 tablespoons of dried marshmallow root, and 1 tablespoon of dried thyme; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and let the mixture simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Remove from heat, add 1 tablespoon of dried mullein leaves, and let it steep for an additional 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove solids, then add honey to taste while the tea is still warm.
Drink the tea throughout the day, up to three times daily, to help alleviate phlegm.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the marshmallow plant contain mucilage, which helps soothe irritated throats and loosen phlegm.
Leaves: The leaves of the marshmallow plant also contain mucilage and have anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in treating phlegm.
Flowers: The flowers of the marshmallow plant contain flavonoids and polysaccharides, which have expectorant and anti-tussive properties, making them useful in treating phlegm.
Seeds: The seeds of the marshmallow plant are rich in mucilage and have a demulcent effect, making them helpful in soothing irritated throats and reducing phlegm.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Great mullein tea helps with phlegm because it contains mucilage, a substance that coats and soothes mucous membranes, making it an effective remedy for respiratory conditions such as bronchitis, asthma, and coughs. The tea's anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties help to loosen and expel phlegm from the lungs, providing relief from congestion and difficulty breathing. Additionally, mullein tea contains saponins, which have been shown to have a cleansing effect on the respiratory tract, further aiding in the elimination of phlegm. Overall, great mullein tea is a natural and gentle way to alleviate symptoms of respiratory conditions and promote respiratory health.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Great mullein tea helps to loosen and expel phlegm from the respiratory tract due to its mucilaginous constituents, such as polysaccharides, which provide a soothing and slippery texture.
Anti-inflammatory: The tea's ability to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, thanks to its flavonoid and saponin content, helps alleviate irritation and swelling associated with phlegm.
Antitussive: Great mullein tea can help suppress coughing fits by soothing the throat and reducing inflammation, which is particularly beneficial for phlegm-related coughs.
Antimicrobial: The tea's antimicrobial properties, derived from rotenoids and other phenolic compounds, can help fight off infections that may contribute to phlegm production.
Demulcent: Great mullein tea creates a protective film over mucous membranes, reducing irritation and providing relief from phlegm-related discomfort, due to its high mucilage content.
remedy_recipe: In a stovetop kettle, boil 1 quart of water.
Add 2-3 teaspoons of dried mullein leaves to a tea infuser or teapot.
Once water is boiling, pour it over the mullein leaves.
Allow the tea to steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the mullein leaves, and enjoy.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of great mullein are commonly used in medicinal teas for phlegm due to their expectorant and soothing properties, which help to clear mucus from the respiratory tract.

Flowers: The bright yellow flowers of great mullein are often used in teas for phlegm due to their anti-inflammatory and mild diuretic properties, which can help reduce inflammation and support the elimination of excess fluids from the body.
Seeds: Mullein seeds are sometimes included in teas for phlegm due to their demulcent properties, which can help soothe and protect irritated membranes in the respiratory tract.

Stems: Although less commonly used than the leaves and flowers, the stems of great mullein can also be included in medicinal teas for phlegm due to their mild expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties.

Roots: While not as commonly used as other parts of the plant, the roots of great mullein can also be used in teas for phlegm due to their demulcent and mildly astringent properties, which can help soothe and tighten irritated tissues in the respiratory tract.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Plantain tea helps with phlegm because it contains mucilage, a substance that becomes slippery when it comes into contact with water. This mucilage coats the throat and helps to soothe irritation, reducing the urge to cough and clear phlegm. Additionally, plantain tea has anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, which help to break up and expel phlegm from the respiratory tract. The herb also contains antioxidants that help to reduce inflammation and support the immune system, making it an effective natural remedy for respiratory conditions such as bronchitis, asthma, and the common cold. Drinking plantain tea can provide relief from phlegm and promote overall respiratory health.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Plantain tea helps to loosen and expel phlegm from the respiratory tract, making it easier to cough up and expel mucus. This property is important for phlegm because it helps to clear airways and reduce congestion, allowing for easier breathing. The mucilage content in plantain leaves gives it this expectorant property.

Anti-inflammatory: Plantain tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating symptoms of phlegm. This property is important for phlegm because inflammation can exacerbate phlegm production and make it more difficult to breathe. The flavonoids and iridoid glycosides in plantain leaves contribute to its anti-inflammatory effects.

Antimicrobial: Plantain tea has antimicrobial properties that can help fight off infections that can cause phlegm. This property is important for phlegm because infections can lead to excessive mucus production and respiratory distress. The antibacterial and antiviral properties of plantain leaves are attributed to its tannins, saponins, and phenolic acids.

Demulcent: Plantain tea has a soothing effect on the mucous membranes, reducing irritation and inflammation. This property is important for phlegm because it can help alleviate symptoms of a cough and sore throat. The mucilage content in plantain leaves gives it this demulcent property.

Antitussive: Plantain tea has antitussive properties that can help suppress a cough, reducing the amount of phlegm that is expelled. This property is important for phlegm because excessive coughing can exacerbate inflammation and cause further respiratory distress. The flavonoids and iridoid glycosides in plantain leaves contribute to its antitussive effects.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 large plantain leaves from a healthy plant, rinse them thoroughly, and let them dry.
Tear the leaves into small pieces, place them in a pot, and add 4 cups of water.
Bring the water to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the plant material, and let it cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Drink 1-2 cups of the herbal plantain tea daily to help alleviate phlegm.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in vitamins and minerals, plantain leaves help to soothe irritation and inflammation in the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel phlegm.
Seeds: High in mucilage content, plantain seeds help to lubricate and soothe mucous membranes, aiding in the expulsion of phlegm.
Roots: Containing expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties, plantain roots help to break up and remove phlegm from the respiratory tract.
Flowers: Packed with antioxidants, plantain flowers help to reduce inflammation and support the body's natural ability to expel phlegm.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: English ivy tea helps with phlegm because it contains saponins, which have expectorant properties that can help loosen and break up mucus in the respiratory tract. Additionally, the tea's anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects can soothe irritated airways and reduce coughing, making it easier to expel phlegm. Furthermore, the tea's antibacterial and antiviral properties can help fight off infections that may be causing phlegm production in the first place. Drinking English ivy tea can provide relief for those suffering from respiratory conditions such as bronchitis, asthma, and COPD.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: English ivy tea helps to loosen and expel phlegm from the respiratory tract due to the presence of saponins, which have expectorant properties.
Anti-inflammatory: The tea's ability to reduce inflammation in the airways, which can help alleviate phlegm congestion, is attributed to its flavonoids and phenolic acids.
Antimicrobial: English ivy tea contains antimicrobial compounds, such as Hederasaponin B and alpha-hederin, which can help combat infections that may cause phlegm production.
Bronchodilator: The tea's bronchodilator properties, which widen the airways and facilitate phlegm expulsion, are attributed to its saponins and essential oils.
Demulcent: English ivy tea creates a soothing film on mucous membranes, reducing irritation and inflammation that can lead to phlegm production, thanks to its mucilage content.
remedy_recipe: Gather 2-3 fresh English ivy leaves and rinse them thoroughly under running water to remove dirt and bacteria.
Chop the leaves finely and place them in a pot, then add 2 cups of water and bring the mixture to a boil.
Reduce the heat to low, cover the pot, and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes to allow the active compounds to be extracted.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the leaves, and let it cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Consume the tea 2-3 times a day, especially before bedtime, to help loosen phlegm and soothe respiratory symptoms.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Thyme tea helps with phlegm because it has antiseptic, antibacterial, and expectorant properties that work together to soothe a sore throat and expel mucus from the respiratory tract. Thyme contains compounds like thymol and carvacrol, which have been shown to have antimicrobial effects against various respiratory pathogens. Furthermore, thyme tea can help stimulate the production of saliva and mucus, making it easier to expel phlegm from the lungs and throat. Its soothing and anti-inflammatory properties can also help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract and provide relief from coughing and other respiratory symptoms. Overall, thyme tea is a natural and effective way to help manage phlegm and support respiratory health.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Thyme tea helps to loosen and expel phlegm from the respiratory tract, making it easier to cough up, due to its active constituents such as flavonoids and volatile oils like thymol and carvacrol.
Antispasmodic: It helps to relax the muscles of the trachea and bronchi, reducing coughing and making breathing easier, thanks to its calming properties found in its volatile oils.

Antimicrobial: Thyme tea inhibits the growth of bacteria and viruses that can cause respiratory infections, thus reducing phlegm production, owing to its potent antimicrobial agents like thymol and carvacrol.
Anti-inflammatory: It reduces inflammation in the airways, alleviating irritation and swelling that can exacerbate phlegm buildup, due to the presence of flavonoids and phenolic acids.

Antioxidant: Thyme tea neutralizes harmful free radicals, protecting lung tissue from damage and supporting overall respiratory health, as a result of its high antioxidant content, including rosmarinic acid and various flavonoids.
remedy_recipe: In a small pot, boil 1 cup of water for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 tablespoon of dried thyme leaves to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let the mixture simmer for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the thyme leaves.
Sip the hot tea slowly to soothe a phlegmy throat.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Thyme leaves are often used in medicinal teas for phlegm due to their natural expectorant properties, which help to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract.
Flowers: Thyme flowers contain essential oils and flavonoids that have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making them useful for soothing irritated throats and alleviating phlegm.

Aerial parts: While not a specific part, aerial parts of thyme (the above-ground parts of the plant, excluding the flowers) are often used in teas for phlegm due to their high concentration of volatile oils, which have been shown to have mucolytic and spasmolytic effects.

Essential oil: Although not a physical part of the plant, the essential oil extracted from thyme is a common ingredient in medicinal teas for phlegm. This oil contains compounds like thymol and carvacrol, which have been shown to have antiseptic, antispasmodic, and expectorant properties.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Licorice tea helps with phlegm because it contains a compound called glycyrrhizin, which has expectorant properties. This means that it can help thin and loosen mucus in the throat, making it easier to cough up and expel. Additionally, licorice tea has anti-inflammatory and soothing effects, which can help reduce irritation and swelling in the respiratory tract. As a result, drinking licorice tea can provide relief from congestion, coughing, and other symptoms associated with phlegm. It is important to note that while licorice tea can be helpful for managing phlegm, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment, especially for chronic or severe respiratory conditions.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Licorice tea helps loosen and thin mucus, making it easier to cough up and expel phlegm. This is due to the presence of glycyrrhizin, a compound that stimulates mucus production and has anti-inflammatory effects.

Demulcent: Licorice tea forms a soothing film over mucous membranes, providing relief from irritation and inflammation in the respiratory tract. The polysaccharides and flavonoids in licorice are responsible for this demulcent effect.
Antitussive: Licorice tea can help suppress coughing, which can be beneficial for managing phlegm. The compound liquiritigenin, found in licorice, has been shown to have antitussive properties.

Antimicrobial: Licorice tea inhibits the growth of bacteria and viruses that can contribute to phlegm production. The flavonoids and triterpenoids in licorice, such as liquiritin and glycyrrhizic acid, have antimicrobial properties.

Anti-inflammatory: Licorice tea reduces inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating symptoms associated with phlegm. Glycyrrhizin, a triterpene saponin, is primarily responsible for the anti-inflammatory effects of licorice.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water and 1 tablespoon of dried licorice root.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Remove the pot from heat and strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solids.
Add honey to taste, if desired, for added sweetness and soothing properties.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day, to help alleviate phlegm.
remedy_parts: Roots: Licorice roots are commonly used in medicinal teas for phlegm due to their expectorant and demulcent properties, which help soothe irritated throats and expel mucus.

Rhyzomes: Licorice rhyzomes are rich in glycyrrhizin, which has been shown to reduce inflammation and help alleviate symptoms of respiratory ailments, making them a popular choice for phlegm-reducing teas.
Seeds: Licorice seeds contain a high concentration of antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, which can help break down phlegm and relieve congestion when used in medicinal teas.

Flowers: Licorice flowers have been used in traditional medicine to treat respiratory conditions, as they contain flavonoids and other compounds that can help reduce inflammation and break down phlegm.
Bark: The bark of the licorice plant is often used in teas for phlegm due to its soothing and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help ease coughing and irritation.

The tea also has anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, which can help reduce inflammation and fight off infections that may contribute to phlegm production. Furthermore, cowslip tea is a natural diuretic, which can help flush toxins and excess fluids from the body, including those that can contribute to phlegm buildup. Drinking cowslip tea can provide relief for those experiencing symptoms of a cold, flu, or other respiratory conditions associated with phlegm.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Cowslip tea helps to loosen and expel phlegm from the respiratory tract, making it easier to cough up and expel. This property is important for phlegm because it aids in the removal of excess mucus and reduces congestion. The expectorant effect is attributed to the saponins and mucilage present in cowslip.

Anti-inflammatory: Cowslip tea has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating irritation and swelling. This property is important for phlegm because it helps soothe inflamed airways, making it easier to breathe and expel phlegm. The anti-inflammatory effect is primarily due to the flavonoids and phenolic acids found in cowslip.

Antispasmodic: Cowslip tea has antispasmodic properties that help relax the muscles in the respiratory tract, reducing coughing and spasms. This property is important for phlegm because it helps alleviate persistent coughing and reduces the strain on the respiratory system. Antimicrobial: Cowslip tea has antimicrobial properties that help inhibit the growth of bacteria and viruses, reducing the risk of respiratory infections. Demulcent: Cowslip tea has demulcent properties that help soothe and protect the mucous membranes in the respiratory tract. remedy_recipe: Chop 2 tablespoons of dried cowslip herb and place it in a teapot.
Boil 1 cup of water and let it cool for 2 minutes.
Pour the hot water over the chopped cowslip herb in the teapot.
Cover the teapot and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the herb, and enjoy the warm beverage up to three times a day.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Cowslip flowers are used in medicinal teas for phlegm due to their anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties.
Leaves: Cowslip leaves are often included in phlegm-relieving teas because of their soothing and mucus-clearing effects.
Roots: The roots of the cowslip plant are used in teas for phlegm due to their natural expectorant and antitussive properties.
Rhizomes: Cowslip rhizomes are included in medicinal teas for phlegm as they contain saponins, which help to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Coltsfoot tea helps with phlegm because it contains mucilage, a substance that coats and soothes the throat, making it easier to expel phlegm. The herb also has expectorant properties, which help to loosen and thin mucus, making it easier to cough up and expel from the lungs. Additionally, coltsfoot contains anti-inflammatory compounds that can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, further helping to alleviate congestion and phlegm. Drinking coltsfoot tea can provide relief for those suffering from respiratory conditions such as bronchitis, asthma, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) by helping to break up and remove phlegm from the lungs.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Coltsfoot tea helps to loosen and expel phlegm from the respiratory tract due to its expectorant properties, which can be attributed to its mucilaginous and saponin contents.

Anti-inflammatory: The tea's anti-inflammatory properties, derived from flavonoids and essential oils, help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, providing relief from phlegm-related irritations.
Antitussive: Coltsfoot tea can help suppress coughs by soothing the throat and reducing the cough reflex, thanks to its antitussive constituents like alkaloids and mucilage.
Demulcent: The tea's demulcent properties, provided by mucilage and polysaccharides, form a protective film over mucous membranes, helping to alleviate irritation and reduce phlegm production.

Antimicrobial: Coltsfoot tea's antimicrobial properties, owing to the presence of tannins and volatile oils, can help inhibit the growth of bacteria and viruses that may exacerbate phlegm production and respiratory infections.
remedy_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water and let it cool down to 90°F (32°C).
Crush 2 teaspoons of dried coltsfoot leaves and add them to the warm water.
Cover and let the mixture steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves and discard them.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day, to help loosen phlegm.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the coltsfoot plant are commonly used in medicinal teas for phlegm due to their expectorant properties, which help to clear mucus from the respiratory tract.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the coltsfoot plant are used in teas for phlegm because they contain mucilage, which helps to soothe irritated membranes and reduce coughing.
Leaves: The leaves of the coltsfoot plant are often used in teas for phlegm due to their demulcent properties, which help to protect and soothe irritated membranes in the respiratory tract.

Flowers: The flowers of the coltsfoot plant contain flavonoids, which have anti-inflammatory properties and can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, making them useful in teas for phlegm.
Seeds: The seeds of the coltsfoot plant contain essential oils, which have expectorant properties and can help to loosen and expel phlegm from the respiratory tract.

Buds: The buds of the coltsfoot plant are rich in antioxidants, which can help to protect the respiratory tract from damage caused by free radicals and inflammation, making them useful in teas for phlegm.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: High mallow tea helps with phlegm because it contains mucilage, a substance that coats and soothes mucous membranes, making it effective in reducing inflammation and irritation in the respiratory tract. This action helps to loosen and expel phlegm, providing relief from congestion and cough. Additionally, high mallow tea has expectorant properties, which further aid in the elimination of phlegm from the lungs and airways. Anti-inflammatory: Its anti-inflammatory properties, due to the presence of flavonoids and phenolic acids, help reduce irritation and swelling in the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel phlegm.
Antitussive: High mallow tea can help suppress coughing, as it contains coumarins that have a calming effect on the cough reflex, alleviating the need to cough up phlegm.
Demulcent: The demulcent properties, attributed to the high mucilage content, form a protective film over mucous membranes, providing relief from phlegm-related irritations.

Add 2 tablespoons of dried high mallow leaves to the boiling water.

remedy_parts: Roots: High mallow roots are commonly used in medicinal teas for phlegm due to their expectorant and demulcent properties, which help soothe irritated throats and expel mucus.
Leaves: High mallow leaves are often used in teas for phlegm because they contain mucilage, which coats and soothes mucous membranes, reducing irritation and inflammation.
Flowers: High mallow flowers are included in teas for phlegm because they have anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, helping to reduce inflammation and expel mucus from the respiratory tract.
Seeds: High mallow seeds are used in teas for phlegm due to their high mucilage content, which helps to lubricate and soothe irritated mucous membranes, making it easier to expel phlegm.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Elderberry tea helps with phlegm because it contains bioflavonoids, antioxidants, and vitamins that have anti-inflammatory and immune-boosting properties. These compounds can help soothe inflammation in the respiratory tract, reduce phlegm production, and alleviate symptoms of congestion and coughing. Additionally, elderberry tea has been shown to have antiviral and antibacterial effects, which can help fight off respiratory infections that can cause phlegm buildup. The warm liquid of the tea can also help loosen and expel phlegm from the throat and lungs, providing relief and promoting faster recovery.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Elderberry tea helps to loosen and expel phlegm from the respiratory tract by stimulating the production of mucus, making it easier to cough up and expel phlegm. This property is important for phlegm because it helps to clear the airways and reduce congestion. The active constituents responsible for this property include flavonoids, tannins, and essential oils.

Anti-inflammatory: Elderberry tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce swelling and irritation in the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe and reducing the production of phlegm. This property is important for phlegm because it can help reduce the underlying causes of phlegm production. The active constituents responsible for this property include flavonoids, anthocyanins, and phenolic acids.

Antiviral: Elderberry tea has antiviral properties that can help to fight off viral infections that can cause phlegm production. This property is important for phlegm because it can help to treat the underlying cause of phlegm production and reduce the severity and duration of symptoms. The active constituents responsible for this property include anthocyanins, flavonoids, and lectins.

Antioxidant: Elderberry tea has antioxidant properties that can help to protect the respiratory tract from damage caused by free radicals, reducing inflammation and phlegm production. This property is important for phlegm because it can help to reduce the underlying causes of phlegm production and support overall respiratory health. The active constituents responsible for this property include anthocyanins, flavonoids, and phenolic acids.

Diuretic: Elderberry tea has diuretic properties that can help to increase urine production, flushing toxins and excess fluids from the body, including phlegm. This property is important for phlegm because it can help to reduce the overall amount of phlegm in the body and support respiratory health. The active constituents responsible for this property include flavonoids, tannins, and essential oils.
remedy_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 2 cups of water, 2 tablespoons of dried elderberries, and 1 cinnamon stick; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat, cover, and simmer the mixture for 30 minutes.
Remove from heat and let it steep for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a teapot or mug.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired, and drink while warm.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Elderberry flowers are rich in antioxidants and have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce phlegm and soothe respiratory tract irritation.
Leaves: Elderberry leaves contain tannins, which can help dry up phlegm and provide relief from congestion.
Bark: The bark of elderberry plants contains high levels of flavonoids, which can help reduce inflammation and break up phlegm.
Seeds: Elderberry seeds are rich in vitamins and minerals, which can help boost the immune system and reduce phlegm production.
Berries: Elderberries are high in antioxidants and have antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce phlegm and soothe a sore throat.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat phlegm with herbal teas is by choosing teas with expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties. Look for herbal ingredients such as licorice root, ginger, thyme, eucalyptus, and slippery elm. These herbs help soothe irritated throats, reduce inflammation, and break up mucus, making it easier to expel. Drink these teas two to three times a day for optimal relief. Remember to consult a healthcare professional before using herbal teas if you are pregnant, breastfeeding, or have any pre-existing medical conditions.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to phlegm that are treated with herbal teas include respiratory conditions such as bronchitis, asthma, and sinus congestion. Herbal teas like thyme, eucalyptus, and ginger are known for their anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, helping to soothe irritated throat and airways, reduce inflammation, and loosen up mucus. Peppermint tea is also commonly used to alleviate symptoms of congestion and cough, while chamomile tea can help promote relaxation and better sleep. However, it is important to consult with a healthcare professional before starting any herbal remedy, especially for pregnant women, children, and individuals with pre-existing medical conditions.
